content.text: Clicking through the Grafana UI to build dashboards produces undocumented, unreviewed, unreproducible configuration. The dashboard-as-code pattern solves this: dashboards are defined in version-controlled files, reviewed in pull requests, and deployed by CI — the same workflow used for application code. For Claude integrations, this means your golden-signal, cost, and quality dashboards evolve alongside the application code that feeds them.

This document covers the full workflow: choosing a definition format, structuring the repository, writing CI steps, deploying via the Grafana API, and preventing configuration drift between Git and production.

attrs.alt: Dashboard-as-Code GitOps pipeline and repository layout

content.text: Two formats dominate the Grafana ecosystem:

For Claude observability dashboards, Jsonnet + Grafonnet is the recommended choice: it enables shared panel libraries (reuse the same time-series template across golden-signal, cost, and quality dashboards) and diffs cleanly in pull requests.
If your team already uses Terraform, the
resource is equally valid — pick the tool your team maintains, not the 'correct' one.
Keep dashboard sources in a dedicated directory within your application repository (or in a separate observability repo shared across services):

dashboards/
├── lib/
│   ├── panels.libsonnet       # reusable panel builders (timeSeries, stat, table)
│   ├── targets.libsonnet      # PromQL target builders for common Claude metrics
│   └── defaults.libsonnet     # standard time ranges, refresh intervals, grid positions
├── claude/
│   ├── golden_signals.jsonnet # latency, traffic, errors, saturation
│   ├── cost_overview.jsonnet  # token cost by model and feature
│   └── quality_signals.jsonnet
├── rendered/                  # auto-generated JSON (committed, used for diffs)
│   └── claude/
│       ├── golden_signals.json
│       ├── cost_overview.json
│       └── quality_signals.json
├── Makefile                   # convenience targets: render, lint, diff, push
└── .github/
    └── workflows/
        └── dashboards.yaml    # CI/CD pipeline
directory holds the last CI-rendered JSON. Committing it enables
to show exactly which panel titles, queries, or thresholds changed in a pull request, without requiring reviewers to understand Jsonnet.
A minimal Grafonnet dashboard for Claude's golden signals:
Render this to JSON with the

# Install jsonnet and Grafonnet
brew install jsonnet       # macOS
# or: go install github.com/google/go-jsonnet/cmd/jsonnet@latest

# Clone Grafonnet as a vendored library
mkdir -p dashboards/vendor
git submodule add https://github.com/grafana/grafonnet-lib dashboards/vendor/grafonnet

# Render
jsonnet -J dashboards/vendor dashboards/claude/golden_signals.jsonnet \
  > dashboards/rendered/claude/golden_signals.json
The pipeline runs on every pull request (lint + render + diff) and on every push to main (deploy):

# .github/workflows/dashboards.yaml
name: Dashboards

on:
  pull_request:
    paths: ['dashboards/**']
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ['dashboards/**']

jobs:
  lint-render:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          submodules: recursive  # vendor/grafonnet

      - name: Install jsonnet
        run: |
          wget -q https://github.com/google/go-jsonnet/releases/download/v0.20.0/go-jsonnet_0.20.0_linux_amd64.tar.gz
          tar -xz -C /usr/local/bin go-jsonnet_0.20.0_linux_amd64.tar.gz jsonnet

      - name: Format check
        run: |
          find dashboards -name '*.jsonnet' -o -name '*.libsonnet' | \
            xargs -I{} jsonnet fmt --test {}

      - name: Render all dashboards
        run: make render-all

      - name: Check rendered diff
        run: |
          git diff --name-only dashboards/rendered/ || true
          git diff --stat dashboards/rendered/
          # Fail if rendered files were not committed after the jsonnet change
          git diff --exit-code dashboards/rendered/ || \
            (echo "::error::Rendered JSON is out of sync. Run 'make render-all' and commit." && exit 1)

  deploy:
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    needs: lint-render
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Push dashboards to Grafana
        env:
          GRAFANA_URL: ${{ secrets.GRAFANA_URL }}
          GRAFANA_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GRAFANA_API_TOKEN }}
        run: |
          for file in dashboards/rendered/claude/*.json; do
            uid=$(jq -r '.uid' "$file")
            echo "Deploying $uid from $file"
            curl -sS --fail -X POST "$GRAFANA_URL/api/dashboards/db" \
              -H "Authorization: Bearer $GRAFANA_TOKEN" \
              -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
              -d "{
                \"dashboard\": $(cat \"$file\"),
                \"overwrite\": true,
                \"message\": \"Deployed by CI from commit $GITHUB_SHA\"
              }"
          done
Use a Grafana service account token (not a user API key) for CI deployments. Scope it to the minimum role needed —
A Makefile wraps the common operations so engineers do not need to memorise the jsonnet flags or Grafana API calls:

JSONNET_FLAGS := -J dashboards/vendor
RENDERED     := dashboards/rendered
SOURCES      := $(shell find dashboards -name '*.jsonnet' -not -path '*/vendor/*' -not -path '*/lib/*')
RENDERED_FILES := $(patsubst dashboards/%.jsonnet,$(RENDERED)/%.json,$(SOURCES))

.PHONY: render-all lint diff push clean

render-all: $(RENDERED_FILES)

$(RENDERED)/%.json: dashboards/%.jsonnet
	@mkdir -p $(dir $@)
	jsonnet $(JSONNET_FLAGS) $< > $@
	@echo "Rendered $@"

lint:
	find dashboards -name '*.jsonnet' -o -name '*.libsonnet' | \
	  grep -v vendor | xargs -I{} jsonnet fmt --test {}

diff:
	@git diff --stat $(RENDERED)/

push:
	@test -n "$(GRAFANA_URL)"   || (echo "GRAFANA_URL not set" && exit 1)
	@test -n "$(GRAFANA_TOKEN)" || (echo "GRAFANA_TOKEN not set" && exit 1)
	for file in $(RENDERED)/claude/*.json; do \
	  uid=$$(jq -r '.uid' "$$file"); \
	  echo "Pushing $$uid"; \
	  curl -sS --fail -X POST "$(GRAFANA_URL)/api/dashboards/db" \
	    -H "Authorization: Bearer $(GRAFANA_TOKEN)" \
	    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
	    -d "{"dashboard": $$(cat $$file), "overwrite": true}" ; \
	done

clean:
	rm -rf $(RENDERED)/

The most common failure mode: an engineer makes a quick UI edit in Grafana to fix an alert threshold during an incident and forgets to commit the change. The next CI deploy overwrites it. Three controls prevent this:
in every managed dashboard JSON. This prevents UI edits without removing view access. Engineers who need to experiment can duplicate to a personal folder.
A nightly CI job fetches all managed dashboards from the Grafana API and diffs them against
. Any divergence triggers a Slack alert.
) rather than letting Grafana auto-generate them. This makes the API URL stable and prevents duplicate dashboards on re-deploy.

# scripts/check_dashboard_drift.py — run nightly
import os, json, requests, subprocess, sys

GRAFANA_URL   = os.environ["GRAFANA_URL"]
GRAFANA_TOKEN = os.environ["GRAFANA_TOKEN"]
RENDERED_DIR  = "dashboards/rendered"


def fetch_dashboard(uid: str) -> dict:
    resp = requests.get(
        f"{GRAFANA_URL}/api/dashboards/uid/{uid}",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {GRAFANA_TOKEN}"},
        timeout=10,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()["dashboard"]


def compare_dashboard(local_path: str) -> bool:
    with open(local_path) as f:
        local = json.load(f)
    uid = local.get("uid")
    if not uid:
        print(f"[skip] {local_path}: no uid")
        return True
    live = fetch_dashboard(uid)
    # Compare only the panels and targets, not version/updated timestamps
    drifted = local.get("panels") != live.get("panels")
    if drifted:
        print(f"[DRIFT] {uid} — local panels differ from Grafana production")
    return not drifted


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import glob
    paths = glob.glob(f"{RENDERED_DIR}/**/*.json", recursive=True)
    failures = [p for p in paths if not compare_dashboard(p)]
    if failures:
        print(f"\n{len(failures)} dashboard(s) have drifted. Re-run CI to restore.")
        sys.exit(1)
    print("All dashboards in sync with Grafana.")
Duplicating panel definitions across dashboards leads to inconsistent thresholds and broken queries after metric renames. Extract common patterns into a
Import the library in each dashboard file with
. When a metric is renamed (e.g. from
), you update it in one file and all dashboards regenerate correctly.
Jsonnet dashboards can be unit-tested with the
library, but a simpler approach is to test the rendered JSON structure directly:
Add this test suite to the CI pipeline (before the deploy step) to catch structural errors before they reach production.
Because every deployed version corresponds to a Git commit, rollback is a
followed by CI — exactly the same mechanism used for application rollbacks:
Identify the last good commit in the dashboards/ directory.
— creates a new commit that undoes the change.
Push to main — CI re-renders and re-deploys.
The bad dashboard version remains in Git history for post-incident analysis.
Revert the jsonnet commit; CI redeploys
Run `make push` to restore from rendered/
Triggered by drift detection; CI redeploys on next run or trigger manually
Pin `schemaVersion` in jsonnet; open upgrade ticket

Treating Grafana dashboards as code — stored in Git, rendered deterministically, reviewed in pull requests, deployed by CI — eliminates the configuration drift and knowledge silos that plague click-configured dashboards. For Claude integrations, this means your golden-signal, cost, and quality panels are as reproducible and auditable as the application code they monitor.
Use Jsonnet + Grafonnet for reusable panel libraries; commit rendered JSON for reviewable diffs.
CI on PR renders and diffs; CI on main deploys via the Grafana API.
Set `"editable": false` on all managed dashboards to prevent silent UI drift.
Run a nightly drift detection job that fetches live dashboards and compares them to rendered/.
Rollback is a git revert — no manual Grafana steps required.
